sentences.text: We arrived in London for [INAUDIBLE].
We were given nice clothes to pick out.
We were made to feel well-- special.
We-- we-- we lived in a hostel with other children just like me from that ship that Rabbi [?
Schonfeld ?] brought us.
We were given allowances of money.
And it was the first time I got an allowance since-- I never got on allowance before.
I don't remember whether-- shillings.
Whether it was 20 shilling a month, or whatever.
I don't remember exactly.
But I remember it was enough to go and buy chocolates.
And in those days, we had-- there were rations in London.
And you couldn't just go into a store and buy chocolate.
You-- you-- you had rations.
And you used these rations.
And if you had-- then you-- the money wasn't-- the rations were more-- in other words, restricted than the money.
We had the amount we needed.
We didn't spend a lot.
We didn't have to buy food or anything.
But luxury like chocolate, which I didn't have the whole war.
Since the war, I didn't have a piece of chocolate.
And I was a great [INAUDIBLE] before the war.
And-- and my [INAUDIBLE], even though I didn't have chocolate.
But this was-- I loved chocolate.
It was Nestle.
I remember it was Nestle.
That was made in-- in England.
And I remember I got the-- the coupons.
And I had so much for chocolate.
I could use it.
I had so much.
I had certain amount of money.
The first time we each got according to our ages.
It was very normal life the.
It was nice.
We were supervised-- supervised by-- by-- there was a lady who was the-- the main supervisor, the head supervisor.
And then there was a lady who made sure we went to sleep every night on time.
Her name was Susie.
And she was very kind to me especially.
The first time I remember somebody made me a birthday.
One day I woke up, and she had on a little table, she put a white napkin.
And I remember she put a little jar of cream.
And you know, this was my birthday present.
With something else, I don't remember.
And it was like a special birthday.
The first time I had a birthday since the-- since the-- since ever because I don't even ever remember having a birthday.
You know, and she made-- how did they figure out my birthday?
Because I told them my mother said I was 6 and 1/2 when the war started.
And I was-- it was 6 and 1/2.
It meant that I was May.
So they figured out that was my birthday.
The day maybe is not exact, but the month is.
So they made me a birthday.
It was May.
And-- and I had my very first birthday.
It was very special.
Susie.
And one day, she took me to her home-- a special treat-- to her parents for Shabbos.
And I visited a great, big synagogue.
And I have to mention that in all the years since the war-- since my own synagogue was destroyed in-- in my village.
And since-- in [INAUDIBLE], I didn't-- since then, I didn't see a synagogue where people go to the-- It was the first time I went in London to a synagogue.
And I couldn't believe it that so many Jewish people still go to a synagogue.
It was unbelievable to me.
Because it was the synagogue where-- a great, big one-- where-- where Rabbi [?
Schonfeld ?] was the main-- was the rabbi there.
And it was a big, big, big synagogue.
A lot of people.
It was very-- a lot of seats there.
And people went-- came ever Shabbos.
This was so-- was the first time I saw people going to a shul.
I haven't seen it since-- since I was a little girl.
Or since I saw my mother, my family.
You know, in normal times when we went to shul.
And it was the first time in all the years.
And it was so amazing to me.
I couldn't believe it.
It was amazing.
I couldn't figure out.
The people go every Shabbos to synagogue when you didn't see it for years?
This was the most amazing thing in London that I-- that I experienced.
And Susie, the young-- the lady.
She wasn't married.
She took care-- she saw to it that we went to sleep on time.
And she was kind.
She didn't make us feel like we were the little girls and she was the grownup.
She made us feel-- she was a real nice person.
That was the right-- this was really her-- her-- I think she fulfilled her role so nicely.
We loved her.
We-- we didn't like-- we didn't think she was too stringent or all this.
She was so nice.
And-- and everybody I encountered in London was the-- are the-- were the nicest people that I experienced since the war ended.
It was all very nice.
Yes.
Oh, I was coming-- My father started to write regularly.
And not only did he write [INAUDIBLE].
It was so nice to-- He expressed himself nicer in the letter than he was able to express himself in real.
In other words, the way he wrote was so beautiful to me that I really felt such warmth in those letters.
And once in  a while, he put in money.
And so I had money from my father coming in.
And he-- I had-- I mad more than enough, you know?
I-- I-- So I developed this, remember-- I develop--
Just [INAUDIBLE].
How are you?
Not big letters, but very warm.
He knew how to express himself in writing.
It-- it was beautiful the way he wrote.
And-- and he wrote me regularly.
So I got regular mail from my father.
It was-- everything was slowly coming to-- to closer to normalcy and everything.
But one thing he didn't do right, he didn't prepare me that he got married.
And this was wrong.
He should have prepared me.
Even a letter saying, I got married, wouldn't have been such a shock.
And I came-- and he didn't realize this.
And I got this terrible--
Just before I came.
I was six months in London.
You know, I went only through the summer.
I never experienced fog.
I only experienced the nicest part of the-- it was beautiful summers.
They're not hot.
And it was beautiful summer I experienced.
And-- and I loved-- I liked it very much.
I even-- They took us sightseeing.
We saw the queen.

And I became such a devoted-- you know, in other words, followed the queen that I brought home-- the only one of the precious possession when I came to America was a postcard of the-- of the royal family.
And I-- and, you know, and I asked-- I wasn't used to a queen.
I mean, I never heard of a queen.
A queen and two princesses.
And they were my age.
It was so nice to hear such a normal-- such-- such-- such a [INAUDIBLE] for queen for [INAUDIBLE].
I didn't know the history, but I-- I felt this-- this-- this great feeling of a queen.
And they loved her, the queen.
And every-- even the Jewish-- the subjects, they had such respect.
And-- and I bought a postcard, I remember, of the queen.
I brought it.
I didn't want to part with it.
You know, and anyway, I went to see the palace.
I went to see a museum.
They tried to-- to-- to show me a little bit of London.
The Buckingham Palace.
They took me to see it.
Took me to see a few important sites.
And I even-- I remember the one movie that I loved-- I saw the first movie, beautiful movie, that I saw was And that was an experience because they were showing Vivien Leigh.
It was very-- a nice experience.
It was a beautiful movie with all the pageantry.
And that was a nice movie to see.
It was happy.
It wasn't sad.
So it didn't remind me of sadness.
And everything was slowly getting to like sort of-- getting better for me.
It was-- every day brought me closer to-- to normal, to being normal.
I even developed-- I remember, what means normal?
Like children have-- I didn't experience what-- to be-- to have a childhood.
I lost my childhood.
So the first time I-- I developed a hobby in London.
I started collecting stamps.
And that's when I was first aware of geography through these stamps.
Because when I saw stamps of [INAUDIBLE].
That time, the-- the-- you know, the countries in-- in Africa had different names than they had now.
They were still the old names.
And I remember seeing all these names, and I developed a passion for-- for-- I bought-- either it went for chocolates or it went for stamps, my-- my-- money.
And I had a whole album with stamps.
And you know, I was-- that was my-- my-- my hobby, the first hobby I had since I didn't have a childhood.
You know, I lost my childhood.
So it was the first hobby I had coming to London.
So I started collecting-- I brought the stamp collection to America.
And slowly, slowly-- and we was told, we're gonna go to school.
To the school where Rabbi [?
Schonfeld ?] started.
I started to go to school.
But again, it was interrupted because I was to leave London after six months.
So I started to go to school.
And I had a-- a Hebrew teacher.
And we-- we were on different levels, but they tried to, you know, to talk to us the best way.
To teach us.
You know, we had to be-- we were at different levels with different ages.
So-- but I do remember one thing the teacher told us, which I never-- it made such a big impact.
It made such a big impression on me.
This Rabbi [?
Levy ?] lives in [INAUDIBLE].
And I found him.
I went to see him.
He told me something which first I begin to understand-- unravel what happened to me.
In other words, I try-- until then, I didn't try to understand it.
I just did everything by rote.
But one time, I remember what he was teaching.
And he said to us-- but this I remember.
He said for every-- for everything on this earth, there's a purpose in life.
And I start to think about why all these things happened.
I started to think, why-- why this happened to me or to the other people, such terrible things?
Maybe there's a reason.
And I was thinking.
It was-- it made the best-- the best-- it's-- the best impression.
Nobody ever told me better than that.
In other words, [INAUDIBLE].
They never gave me a better answer.
I think what he said made the most-- most common sense if you try to ask why.
And this teacher said, Rabbi [?
Levy-- ?] you know, he was a tall gentleman.
He-- and I witnessed-- I went to his wedding.
He was married in London.
We were all invited to his wedding.
And I remember that he said to everything, there is a reason.
That-- that even little [NON-ENGLISH], which how do you say [NON-ENGLISH]?
Worms.
Little worms.
But it's not worms.
It's little creatures, even the tiniest creatures on earth.
As a whole, as an army, they make-- they have a-- they have a purpose.
As one, maybe nothing.
But as a whole, that every little [NON-ENGLISH] by itself has a-- a purpose in this earth.

And-- and God has a maybe a great design as a whole-- in other words, even as a person, I figure if a [NON-ENGLISH] has a-- has a reason for why it was created and why it go through the-- through the motion of life, maybe there's a reason why all this happened.
Maybe there's a grand-- God has a grand design.
And I don't understand it.
And maybe there is a purpose.
If-- if a [NON-ENGLISH] is important in this earth, on this earth, as-- as a creation.
So maybe there's a-- so I figured, maybe there is a purpose for me being on this earth in spite of it going through such terrible things.
And maybe there is a reason that I don't understand.
He gave me the best, I think, reason to understand for my existence.
That-- that-- those few words made such an impact.
Years later, many, many years when I found him and I reminded him, he forgot about it.
And I didn't.
And I reminded him, what you said made such a big impact on my life.
You don't have to learn great things, but sometimes somebody says something, it means a great deal for your very existence.
And I met him and his wife.
Also, she was very kind and they were married.
And they have children.
And they live very-- they're-- they're very, very Orthodox, very learned.
And they live in [INAUDIBLE].
And I met them.
So this was the teacher I met in London.
And he made a great impact.
In other words, he made a great impression on my life, even though I wasn't very long in his class.
So this-- I'm-- and then it was time for me, finally, to make papers.
It was time for me to go to America.
And they made a [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, the time came and I had to-- my few belongings I had to pack.
And I remember I went with my own money.
I bought my own valise.
Not the most expensive, but a valise.
I can put my precious possession, whatever it was, in that valise.
And my album, and whatever I had.
And I packed it in.
It was time to go to America.
It was like I can't describe.
It was so excited.
But you know what?
Some of the excitement I had to-- I-- I learned to repress.
So I didn't show it, but I was excited, of course.
But I was very-- outward.
I was very-- I was very subdued.
Very-- didn't show it much, you know?
But everybody else, I think, realized how fortunate, the fact that I did have-- because none of them had a father in America.
But the time came, and-- and I had to go and make papers.
And again, I didn't have papers.
I didn't have no-- no official-- no birth certificate.
They had to make an official-- I should-- in other words, a paper I should be able to go to another country.
So-- so again, I said-- the lady asked me how old.
She said, which year are you born?
I didn't remember.
Didn't know.
But I did remember my mother saying how old I was when the war broke out.
According to that, she figured out exactly 6 and 1/2.
OK, so now you're 12.
You know, and so they put-- I have the document here you can see.
Eyes blue, brown hair.
You know, and she made out the document.
And officially, the first document I had.
And I went, you know, went to America.
The day came and I went-- I remember it was the [?
Cook ?] Agency.
They got the ticket for me.
And I went to America on a-- on a-- on a ship.
And there was a lady there.
And she was going to America.
They-- they got in touch with some lady, a Jewish woman.
I don't know where to find [? her. ?] And she had a girl.
And they put me under-- that she should watch over me on the boat when I was going on the boat-- on the ship.
No, that's the funny-- I don't remember.
I-- I blocked out names.
But I remember-- I have a picture somewhere of her.
I remember she was dark, and she was nicely dressed.
And by the way, they bought me a new coat in London, a brand new blue coat.
And I have a picture of it.
I went to the department store.
And-- and even clothing was on ration.
And I remember-- I remember who-- yeah, I remember from the [?
Levy ?] family, they brought-- they bought me a blue coat, royal blue coat.
It was a really very nice.
It was my own, first my own coat.
The first coat I owned since my own, especially for me, since I-- since my mother made me a coat.
And so I had a new coat.
And there I was going to America.
Yes, I do.
It was-- it was in September.
And I remember I arrived just before Rosh Hashanah.
Before Rosh Hashanah time.
Now, what's-- what I have to bring in here is like this.
Two of my uncles survived from my mother's-- from my mother's-- in other words, there were eight siblings.
My mother was the oldest.
Only two survived.
So I already knew that my uncle survived in Sweden.
That two of my uncles were in Sweden as I passed Sweden.
So I got off in Sweden, because the ship stopped for one day.
And I had his address.
And I was so smart that I remembered a taxi took me all by myself to that address.

And as the taxi pulled near the house where I was supposed to meet my uncle-- the one who I didn't see all the war years and who was my-- you know, my mother's brother, I expected two uncles to meet me.
Instead, I found that one just died before I came as a result of this terrible war.
He survived the concentration camp, and he got sick, very sick with typhus, or whatever.
And he just died.
And my uncle buried him.
And I have the picture of the grave and everything.
And he-- only one uncle came, the younger one, came to meet me.
But what happened was as the taxi pulled in front of the house, the men just went into the house.
And I was supposed to meet him at a certain number inside the house.
But when I saw him walk in the house, I recognized from the back after not seeing him for years.
And I started to scream at the taxi, that's my uncle!
That's my uncle!
I-- from the back, I recognized him.
And I ran after him.
And by the time he went in the house, he disappeared into the elevator, or whatever.
And I didn't know which door he went in.
And I forgot the number.
Anyway, I found him.
And the reunion was unbelievable.
Because, you see, my uncle I did remember before the war-- in the war and before the war.
And this was the only relative that was remained from my mother's family.
And he was so happy to see me that-- I don't know, he showed more emotion maybe than my father.
I don't know.
He was such-- it was unbelievable the emotion that I felt when I saw this uncle.
And we were-- a whole day.
We spent such a nice day.
And unfortunately, on the other hand, he told me his-- my-- my favorite uncle, that he was-- that he died.
He's buried in Stockholm among other young people who-- who died just like him as a result of the war.
He's buried in a certain section in Stockholm.
And many years later, when I went on a trip, I visited the grave of this uncle.
I have a picture of it.
And I look devastated when I saw this grave.
This little stone, that's all that's left.
Such a small stone flush with the ground, that's all that's left of my mother-- of him.
He was never married.
He never had children.
And died as a young-- 22 years old.
He was buried after surviving the-- the terrible-- the terrible war.
But that's it.
He died a normal-- I mean, he was buried.
In other words, he had a funeral.
But-- so this is the story [INAUDIBLE].
So now you go and gone to London-- to United States.
And then I had to leave Sweden.
I had to leave my uncle there because he arrived the next year or a few months after me.
He had-- he didn't get the papers, but I got the paper.
In other words, I arrived before him.
So I had to say goodbye.
And-- and was very-- and then I went back to the ship and the ship sailed to America.
And when I-- you know, I don't remember the whole ship because I was also very seasick.
And I took-- you know-- What was your arrival like to New York?
It was-- it was at night.
It was late at night.
In other words, it was dark and I didn't see it.
And I did not arrive at the Statue of Liberty like some Jewish people previous before me.
I arrived at a dock and my father came to pick me up.
And this is what the-- what the-- I would like to say [? here ?] like this.
All the years I strived to see this father.
[CRYING] I dreamt the day I was gonna see him, when I'm finally going to be reunited with him.
[CRYING] But when I finally saw him, I was very disappointed.
I thought he was gonna take me in his arms, and he's gonna say, my daughter.
I'm so happy that you survived from the three-- from my other siblings, but it wasn't like this.
He just-- when he finally realized it's me, he took me by the hand.
We walked out of the docks.
And there was the stepmother.
And she was-- and he didn't-- I wasn't prepared for this.
And not only that, we didn't have-- they didn't have their own apartment, but they lived with her parents.
So I had to go-- suddenly, not only was I not prepared for a step-- I was unprepared for step parents that I didn't know.
And they-- they were in America.
And they didn't understand what a child like me went through.
Nobody-- in those days, nobody understood what survivors.
In other words, nobody talked about it.
It was-- it was something to be avoided.
You didn't talk about it.
You're made-- made to feel like a [INAUDIBLE].
Like--
The fact that I survived, children in school laughed.
In a way, made fun of you because you didn't speak English.
And so you couldn't right away integrate just like other children.
It was a stigma.
And-- and so it was very hard.
It was very difficult.
Not only I had to start going to school, and I never went to a first grade.
I had to-- I had to-- you would have to be more than a genius to catch up six-- the first six years of arithmetic, English, a strange language when I-- when I started to go to school.
When I realized I have to go to school.
I have to learn ABC at-- you know, I was-- in 1946, I was 13 years old.
A 13-year-old girl feels bashful not to know.
Feels like a dummy not to know the-- the beginnings of arithmetic.
You are taught in the first few grades.
You are taught first addition, subtraction, you know, everything.
And I didn't learn.
I didn't have-- I had to learn how to survive instead of this.
When children learn how to go to school in normal-- they go through the normal channels of grade 1, 2.
First, kindergarten.
And-- and I didn't have any of this.
I didn't have-- so I didn't know arithmetic.
I felt very-- you know, learning, I felt so, you know, inferior.
But-- but I knew that I wasn't stupid.
Because in order to survive, you had to-- you needed to be bright.
So I knew I wasn't stupid, I just wasn't-- I didn't go through the channels of-- of-- of learning.
And this came very difficult.
It was a language I didn't know.
I didn't know the English language.
I never heard it before.
Because it's very different from Polish.
In Polish, the way you-- the way you pronounce it, the way you say it, that's how you write it.
And here, it was just completely different.
It was very difficult.
And I was made to feel ashamed because the other children who knew English made you feel like you are something of a strange thing.
I mean, I couldn't converse.
I couldn't speak English.
And I didn't know what was being said in class.
They put me into the classes.
It's like throwing somebody into the water and saying, sink or swim.
And that was it.
I had to swim.
I had to try to swim.
I didn't know what I was doing in class when the teacher was speaking.
I didn't understand.
They even tried to put me a little higher not-- because they couldn't put me with the first graders, but-- but I didn't understand the work.
And it took me years till I-- slowly, it came.
But the arithmetic, I don't know if I ever caught up.
I don't think I ever-- reading, because I liked reading.
So it-- I caught up more than some other people.
I read a great-- but arithmetic, I never caught up.
History, I caught up.
I know enough of world, everything.
But the arithmetic came the hardest to me.
And that's what I feel inadequate till today.
But somehow, I swam through it like you swim through the end.
I swam through it.
And I look back.
I wouldn't want to go through this again for anything.
I wouldn't want to go through this.
Believe me.
Oh, it was-- it wasn't-- it wasn't easy.
It-- because I explained to you what it was.
My-- the-- the-- the memory of my mother being killed was so terrible.
In other words, if it was-- if she would have died a normal death, I don't think it would have been so bad.
But because she died-- the way she died, there was no closure.
There was no closure to her death.
There was no-- there was no closure.
I didn't have time to mourn.
I never went through the period of mourning.
I never went through the period of childhood.
I never went-- I never experienced a lot of things a lot of people go through normal channels with life.
Even if a parent dies, you-- you-- there's a period of mourning.
You go through the Shiva.
I never went through.
I never-- I had to hide when-- you know, I didn't go through the period of Shiva for my sister.
I didn't experience the Shiva for my grandmother-- or to my mother, in other words, all these things which are supposed to help you.
Shiva is supposed to help you when-- when the time to-- to-- to help you through the mourning period.
I couldn't express it.
And I never had the chance to experience this.
So I think it was all left inside of me.
I think all these feelings, all these unresolved was left in me.
Were you angry at your father?
Not angry.
But-- I was never angry because I understood that part because my mother explained it to me.
And then I asked my father things.
You know, so I was able to-- I was able to come to terms with it better than he.
And I'll explain to you why I know that.
The reason why I think-- I think he felt-- he must have felt guilt feelings.
How do I know that?
That he left-- it wasn't his fault.
But indirectly, the fact that he left and my mother remained and they were killed.
And this-- you know, my mother, my sisters, my grandparents-- I think that he-- I don't think he resolved it.
The way I know is because whenever I try to ask him something, he was-- he wasn't able to deal with this.
He wasn't able to deal with-- by saying to me it was so and so.
He tried to cut me off very quickly.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] he would say in [INAUDIBLE].
But you ask me this.
He couldn't deal with it.
He couldn't deal with it, that's how I know that-- that he had some unresolved feelings that he couldn't come to terms with.
You know?
He never asked me much.
And that was wrong.
Because if-- could he have asked me a lot more, I think I would have gotten out more.
It wasn't enough brought up.
He was sort of like-- he-- maybe he-- like, as if he found out they were killed and he left it at that.
He didn't go into detail.
When did this-- how did you?
Maybe he knew something, but he never-- I don't think he knew how to deal with it.
He never asked me.
For instance, I was with my grandmother, his mother, to the last-- for the last minute.
He never went into that a great deal.
So, you know, and-- So that's how I know that I think he had guilt.
He didn't know how to deal with it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And in the beginning, I don't know if I expressed myself.
Either they were afraid that they're going to touch on terrible memories.
They were shielding me, too.
I think they were trying, maybe to shield me.
Maybe they were afraid that it will make me-- that it will reawaken very bad memories.
And they were shielding me.
Too shielding is also no good.
You know why?
Because-- because-- because I wasn't able to-- to let out my grief.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to repress it.
And I was afraid, like to mention, even my mother because there was the stepmother.
And I'll give you an example.
I had a picture of my mother from my-- from my great aunt.
And I was afraid to show it in the open that my stepmother shouldn't see it.
She shouldn't feel bad that I'm looking at the picture.
So I never put it out till my stepmother died.
That's when I put the picture on the wall.
OK.
